steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what the three underlined phrases — 'like a culprit,' 'giving himself up to justice,' and 'expecting a rebuke' — do together in the passage. Durant has worked the same reliable routine for thirty years, yet these phrases cast him as guilty and bracing for punishment. That contradiction between his stable record and his internal dread is what the phrases convey.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — They illustrate an unexplained nervous tension that Durant experiences as he begins each workday.: Matches precisely — the phrases show anxiety with no stated cause, which contradicts his flawless thirty-year record. ✓
  • Choice B — They suggest Durant's dismay about being trapped in a job that has become exceedingly monotonous.: The phrases express guilt and fear, not boredom or dismay about monotony. ✗
  • Choice C — They reveal that Durant is unwaveringly precise in his daily routine because of the unreasonable expectations of his employers.: The text gives no hint that employer expectations drive his routine or his anxiety. ✗
  • Choice D — They convey Durant's growing apprehensiveness about having failed to complete an important work task for the first time in his long career.: No specific failure is mentioned; his anxiety is habitual and groundless, not tied to a first-time lapse. ✗
That matches Choice A — They illustrate an unexplained nervous tension that Durant experiences as he begins each workday..
